one_line_takeaway: A two-agent LLM framework that iteratively rewrites claims under a strict black-box, 10-query, binary-feedback threat model achieves 19.95-40.34% evasion against modern LLM-based misinformation-detection pipelines (vs. <=3.90% for token-level baselines using a surrogate model) and 97.02% against a legacy lexical-retrieval system, with a text-simplification defense cutting evasion by up to 65.18%.
key_finding: Full-History variant achieved attack success of 40.34% (Verifact), 30.35% (ICL), and 19.95% (Perplexity) on modern LLM-based pipelines (95% Wilson CIs, roughly ±3.5-4.3pp), versus at most 3.90% for the strongest token-level baseline (CLARE, on ICL) and below 2.18% on the other modern targets -- no baseline CI overlapped the framework's CI on any modern target. The non-iterative Attacker-Only ablation alone reached 14.55-35.92% on modern targets, indicating most of the gap over token-level baselines comes from semantic-level (sentence-level) rewriting itself rather than the iterative optimization loop. Prompt optimization's marginal value scaled inversely with target robustness: +2.00pp on the already-brittle ClaimBuster (95.02% -> 97.02%) vs. +10.24pp on the more robust ICL (20.11% -> 30.35%). The legacy lexical-retrieval system ClaimBuster was near-totally exploitable (97.02%), used as an architectural lower bound rather than a headline result. Post-hoc analysis of successful Full-History rewrites identified four correlated (not causally-ablated) exploitation patterns: (1) hedging/ambiguity injection (vocabulary shift toward terms like 'might', 'reportedly', 'potentially'), (2) structural elaboration (text length +28 to 54%, from 103.29 to 132.34-159.46 characters), (3) complexity escalation (Flesch Reading Ease dropped 32-39%, from 64.40 to 39.58-43.48; perplexity dropped from 207.17 to 73.06-110.56), and (4) syntactic restructuring (parse-tree distance 0.3050-0.3287, Levenshtein distance 69.56-85.72, BERTScore 0.8376-0.8550, well above the 0.77 threshold). A GPT-4o-based text-simplification defense targeting Pattern 3 reduced attack success by 65.18% on ICL (30.35%->10.57%), 48.50% on Verifact (40.34%->20.77%), 47.73% on Perplexity (19.95%->10.43%), and 39.93% on ClaimBuster (97.02%->58.28%, still the highest post-defense rate, attributed to ClaimBuster's vulnerability being retrieval-architectural rather than pattern-driven). Cost: ~$0.30-$0.50 per claim (GPT-4o pricing), 45-90s wall-clock per claim for Full History; convergence analysis shows Full History reaches 60-75% of final success within 4 iterations, suggesting a T=5 budget captures most gains.
core_contribution: The paper proposes a two-agent (Attacker Agent + Prompt Optimization Agent) LLM-driven framework for adversarially rewriting text to evade black-box, multi-component NLP pipelines under a strict threat model: only binary decision feedback, no gradients/logits, and a hard budget of 10 queries per input. The Attacker Agent produces meaning-preserving, stylistically transformed rewrites; the Prompt Optimization Agent iteratively revises the Attacker's system instructions using outcomes/constraint-check results from prior attempts (two variants: Full-History context vs. Previous-Only context, plus an ablation 'Attacker Only' variant with no optimization loop). Every candidate rewrite must simultaneously satisfy four constraints: MPNet embedding cosine similarity >= 0.61, BERTScore F1 (deberta-xlarge-mnli) >= 0.77, a GPT-4o binary semantic-equivalence check, and a GPT-4o-mini binary coherence check. The paper also derives and evaluates a pattern-informed defense (GPT-4o-based text simplification) motivated by its own exploitation-pattern analysis.
threat_model: Strict black-box: adversary observes only the target pipeline's binary output f(x) in {0,1}, with no access to gradients, logits, confidence scores, architecture, or training data. The adversary is assumed to know the ground-truth label of the claim it is submitting (operationally realistic for a disinformation actor pushing a false claim toward 'true', or a propagandist pushing a true claim toward 'false'). Attacks are bounded to a query budget of T=10 per input, modeling rate-limited/cost-per-call production APIs. Target systems are evidence-based misinformation-detection pipelines with a canonical three-stage architecture: NL input intake -> NL evidence retrieval -> NL inferential comparison.
limitations: The paper itself lists six limitations: (1) the GPT-4o-based semantic-equivalence check shares model family with the Attacker Agent (same-family bias), only partially mitigated by the two independent embedding-based checks, and the human calibration study (Kappa 1.0 coherence / 0.610 semantic) used validation pairs, not actual attack outputs; (2) evasion rates are single-run point estimates -- Wilson CIs capture sampling variance but not LLM-generation stochasticity, so no multi-seed evaluation was done; (3) evaluation is restricted to one dataset (LIAR-New, political claims) and one domain (evidence-based misinformation detection); generalization to FEVER/AVERITEC or to other multi-stage domains (RAG QA, tool-augmented agents) is untested; (4) no cross-pipeline transferability of adversarial rewrites was evaluated, and the method is restricted to binary-output pipelines; (5) the four exploitation patterns are post-hoc, correlational aggregate-statistic observations, not established via controlled per-pattern ablation; (6) results are tied to a specific commercial API version (GPT-4o) and may not reproduce after model updates.

datasets_used: LIAR-New (1,957 PolitiFact statements; 500-sample stratified subset used for attacks; underlying LIAR six-class labels for the surrogate fine-tuning)
quantitative_results: Attack success (Full History): Verifact 40.34% [±4.3pp], ICL 30.35% [±4.0pp], Perplexity 19.95% [±3.5pp], ClaimBuster 97.02% [±1.5pp] (95% Wilson CI, n=500)
Attack success (Attacker Only, no optimization): ICL 20.11%, Verifact 35.92%, ClaimBuster 95.02%, Perplexity 14.55%
Attack success (Previous Step): ICL 25.00%, Verifact 39.56%, ClaimBuster 93.05%, Perplexity 18.14%
Strongest token-level baseline CLARE: ICL 3.90% [±1.7pp], Verifact 1.46%, ClaimBuster 26.56% [±3.9pp], Perplexity 0.46%
Other baselines (Full History context, best cases across targets): DeepWordBug up to 10.82% (ClaimBuster), TextBugger up to 22.95% (ClaimBuster), TextFooler up to 23.93% (ClaimBuster)
Target pipeline baseline accuracy / Macro F1: Perplexity 86.20% / 68.88%; Verifact 82.40% / 65.11%; ICL 71.20% / 55.00%; ClaimBuster 61.00% / 49.89%
Prompt-optimization marginal gain: +2.00pp on ClaimBuster (95.02%->97.02%) vs. +10.24pp on ICL (20.11%->30.35%)
Defense (text simplification) evasion reduction: ICL 65.18% (30.35%->10.57%), Verifact 48.50% (40.34%->20.77%), Perplexity 47.73% (19.95%->10.43%), ClaimBuster 39.93% (97.02%->58.28%)

Text-property shifts in successful rewrites: character length 103.29 (orig) -> 132.34-159.46 (adv, +28-54%); Flesch Reading Ease 64.40 -> 39.58-43.48 (-32 to -39%); perplexity 207.17 -> 73.06-110.56; BERTScore 0.8376-0.8550; Levenshtein distance 69.56-85.72; parse-tree distance 0.3050-0.3287

Cost/latency: ~$0.30-$0.50 per claim, $150-$250 per 500-claim run per target; 45-90s per claim (Full History), ~30s for highly vulnerable targets via early termination; 60-75% of final success reached within 4 of 10 iterations

Threshold calibration: MPNet cosine similarity threshold 0.61, BERTScore F1 threshold 0.77, derived from 200 human-annotated pairs (3 annotators), Fleiss' Kappa 1.0 (coherence) / 0.610 (semantic equivalence)
baselines_compared: TextFooler (all via TextAttack framework, using a RoBERTa-base surrogate model fine-tuned on LIAR)
